premise.title: Smallness of local squared spin expectation
premise.name: expectation_S_squared_order_epsilon

premises.premise: The expectation value $\langle S^{2}\rangle$ of the local squared spin field $S^{2}(\vec{x})\equiv\sum_{\alpha=1}^{n-1}S_{\alpha}^{2}(\vec{x})$, where $S_{\alpha}(\vec{x})$ are the $n-1$ independent components of a classical $O(n)$ spin field after eliminating the component $S_{n}$ via the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{n}S_{i}^{2}(\vec{x})=1$, in spatial dimension $d=2+\epsilon$ with small positive parameter $\epsilon$ and number of spin components $n>2$, satisfies $\langle S^{2}\rangle=O(\epsilon)$ (i.e., $\langle S^{2}\rangle$ is proportional to $\epsilon$ to leading order), so that when expanding $(1-S^{2})^{1/2}$ in powers of $S^{2}$ it is sufficient to retain terms up to order $S^{2}$ (giving quartic interactions in the Fourier representation) to obtain results correct to first order in $\epsilon$.

premise.title: Negligible Jacobian from eliminating one spin component
premise.name: jacobian_ignored_first_order

premises.premise: For the classical $O(n)$ spin field with components $S_{i}(\vec{x})$, $i=1,\dots,n$, subject to the local constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{n}S_{i}^{2}(\vec{x})=1$, the functional integral Jacobian factor that arises when eliminating the component $S_{n}(\vec{x})$ and expressing the theory in terms of the remaining $n-1$ fields $S_{\alpha}(\vec{x})$ ($\alpha=1,\dots,n-1$) contributes only terms that are of order higher than first order in the small parameter $\epsilon$ in the low-temperature expansion about $d=2+\epsilon$, and therefore this Jacobian can be neglected when deriving the renormalization-group recursion relations to first order in $\epsilon$.

premise.title: Validity of first-order diagrammatic recursion relations
premise.name: diagrammatic_recursions_validity

premises.premise: Consider the Fourier-space effective Hamiltonian for the $n-1$ fields $S_{\alpha}(\vec{k})$ defined on momenta $|\vec{k}|\le 1$ with short-distance cutoff set by unit radius, with quadratic kinetic coefficients $a$ multiplying $k^{2}$ and $b$ multiplying $k^{\sigma}$, temperature $T$ (in energy units), spatial dimension $d=2+\epsilon$ with small $\epsilon$, and long-range exponent $\sigma$ with $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$. The first-order renormalization-group recursion relations for the running couplings $(a/T)$ and $(b/T)$ obtained by integrating out modes in the momentum shell $1/2<q<1$ and rescaling the momenta and fields (with field rescaling exponent $\eta$ chosen to permit nontrivial fixed points) are given in analytical form by the pair of relations denoted in the paper as equations (6) and (7); these relations are obtained by evaluating the one-loop and related diagrams shown in Fig. 1 and by approximating certain shell integrals (e.g., extracting the small-$k$ $k^{2}$ contribution by setting $\sigma=2$ and $d=2$ in the analytic part of the integrand) so that diagrammatic contributions such as $(k^{2}/2\pi)\ln2$ appear. The claim is that these analytic recursion relations correctly capture all contributions at first order in $\epsilon$ necessary to locate fixed points and linearize flows. Fig. 1

The long-range exponent $\sigma$ satisfies the scaling regime condition $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$ where $\epsilon=d-2$ is a small positive parameter, i.e., $\sigma$ is assumed to be close to 2 with the difference between 2 and $\sigma$ being of the same asymptotic order in the expansion parameter $\epsilon$ used in the low-temperature renormalization-group analysis.

premise.title: Multiplicative RG flow for thermal ratio (T/a) at b=0
premise.name: flow_T_over_a_b_zero

premises.premise: For the Fourier-space effective Hamiltonian with coefficients $a$ (for $k^{2}$) and $b=0$ (vanishing long-range coupling), temperature $T$, spatial dimension $d=2+\epsilon$ with small $\epsilon$, and number of spin components $n>2$, the renormalization-group transformation derived from the first-order recursion relations yields the multiplicative flow equation for the combination $(T/a)$ at the order treated: $$ \left(\frac{T}{a}\right)' \;=\; 2^{-\epsilon}\left(\frac{T}{a}\right)\left(1+(n-2)\frac{T}{a}\frac{\ln2}{2\pi}\right), $$ where primes denote the couplings after one RG step; this flow equation is used to linearize about the SR fixed-point value of $(T/a)$ and to extract the thermal eigenvalue that defines the correlation-length exponent $\nu$ to leading order in $\epsilon$.

premise.title: Linearized eigenvalue for long-range perturbation at SR point
premise.name: linearized_long_range_eigenvalue

premises.premise: At the short-range (SR) fixed point characterized by $b^{*}=0$, $(T/a)^{*}=2\pi\epsilon/(n-2)$, and SR anomalous-dimension $\eta_{SR}$ (defined via the RG field rescaling $S_{\alpha}'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S_{\alpha}(\vec{q})$), the linearization of the first-order recursion relations in an infinitesimal long-range perturbation $\delta b/T$ yields the multiplicative RG eigenvalue relation $$ \big(\delta b/T\big)' \;=\; 2^{\,2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}}\;\big(\delta b/T\big), $$ valid to the order in which $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$ is treated; this eigenvalue is used to determine whether the LR perturbation is relevant ($2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}>0$), marginal ($=$0), or irrelevant ($<0$).

premise.title: k^σ coefficient fixes T/(a+b) at LR fixed point
premise.name: T_over_a_plus_b_fixed_point_relation

premises.premise: For the Fourier-space effective Hamiltonian with nonzero long-range coupling $b\neq0$, temperature $T$, coefficients $a$ and $b$ for $k^{2}$ and $k^{\sigma}$ respectively, spatial dimension $d=2+\epsilon$ and number of spin components $n>2$, equating the coefficients of the $k^{\sigma}$ terms in the first-order recursion relations at a fixed point (in which the coefficient of $k^{\sigma}$ is invariant under the RG step) yields the algebraic relation for the LR fixed point: $$ \Big(\frac{T}{\,a+b\,}\Big)^{*} \;=\; \frac{2\pi(d-\sigma)}{\,n-1\,}, $$ where $(\cdot)^{*}$ denotes the fixed-point value; this relation is obtained from the $k^{\sigma}$ sector of the diagrammatic recursion relations to the order treated.

premise.title: k^2 coefficient yields b/a* involving eta_SR
premise.name: b_over_a_fixed_point_relation

premises.premise: Using the fixed-point value of $T/(a+b)$ obtained from the $k^{\sigma}$ coefficient and substituting into the $k^{2}$ coefficient relation of the first-order recursion relations for the case $b\neq0$, one obtains at the LR fixed point the algebraic expression for the ratio of the long-range to short-range stiffness coefficients, $$ \Big(\frac{b}{a}\Big)^{*} \;=\; \frac{n-2}{\,d-\sigma\,}\big(2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}\big), $$ where $d=2+\epsilon$, $n>2$, $\eta_{SR}$ is the short-range anomalous-dimension exponent evaluated at the SR fixed point, and this expression is obtained by solving the $k^{2}$ sector algebraic relation to the order treated.

For the low-temperature renormalization-group treatment of the classical $O(n)$ ferromagnet in $d=2+\epsilon$ with quadratic short-range coefficient $a$ and long-range coefficient $b$, the truncation of the RG description to the two coupling combinations $T/a$ and $T/b$ (or equivalently $T/(a+b)$ and $b/a$) is self-consistent to the first order in $\epsilon$ and $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$, in the sense that higher-order couplings generated by eliminating short-wavelength modes (e.g., six-point and higher interactions) do not change the first-order fixed-point coordinates $(T/(a+b))^{*}$ and $(b/a)^{*}$ obtained from the $k^{\sigma}$ and $k^{2}$ sectors of the recursion relations, and elimination of $b^{*}$ between those two relations reproduces the $k^{2}$ recursion relation result for $(T/a)^{*}$.

premise.title: Multiplicative RG flow for T/b at nonzero b
premise.name: flow_T_over_b_nonzero_b

premises.premise: For the Fourier-space effective Hamiltonian with nonzero long-range coefficient $b\neq0$, temperature $T$, coefficients $a$ and $b$, spatial dimension $d=2+\epsilon$, and long-range exponent $\sigma$, the first-order recursion relations yield the multiplicative renormalization-group flow equation for the thermal combination $(T/b)$ of the form $$ \left(\frac{T}{b}\right)^{\prime}=2^{-d+\sigma}\left(\frac{T}{b}\right)\left(1+(\pi-1)\frac{T}{b}\frac{b}{a+b}\frac{\ln2}{2\pi}\right), $$ where primes denote the values after one RG step; this flow equation is used to linearize about the LR fixed point and to extract the leading-order thermal eigenvalue that defines $\nu_{\mathrm{LR}}$.

contexts.context: The renormalization-group transformation used in the analysis is defined by integrating out Fourier modes with wave vectors in the shell $1/2<q<1$ (where the unit of inverse length is set by the short-distance cutoff) and then rescaling momenta by $q'=2q\in[0,1]$ together with a field rescaling $S_{\alpha}'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S_{\alpha}(\vec{q})$, where $\eta$ is an adjustable anomalous-dimension parameter chosen so that repeated application of this RG transformation can produce a nontrivial fixed-point Hamiltonian rather than driving parameters to trivial limits.

context.title: Short-distance cutoff and unit momentum sphere
context.name: short_distance_cutoff_unit_sphere

contexts.context: All momentum-space integrals in the derivation are regulated by a short-distance cutoff implemented as an upper bound on wave-vector magnitude; the integration regions are restricted to the ball $|\vec{k}|\le 1$ (the radius of this ball is chosen as the unit of inverse length defining the cutoff), and the shell integrated out in the RG step is $1/2<|\vec{p}|<1$.

context.title: Use of analytic expansion for shell integrals
context.name: analytic_expansion_shell_integrals

contexts.context: When evaluating one-loop and related shell integrals appearing in the diagrammatic recursion relations, expressions of the form $\int_{1/2<p<1}d^{d}p\,\frac{a|\vec{k}-\vec{p}|^{2}+b|\vec{k}-\vec{p}|^{\sigma}}{a p^{2}+b p^{\sigma}}$ are asserted to be analytic in the external momentum $\vec{k}$ for small $|\vec{k}|$ and expanded as $A+B k^{2}+\cdots$, and in evaluating the diagrams to first order the authors set $\sigma=2$ and $d=2$ inside the analytic part to extract the $k^{2}$ coefficient (producing terms such as $(k^{2}/2\pi)\ln2$); this analytic-expansion-and-evaluation step is used as an intermediate technical approximation in the diagram calculations.

conclusions.conclusion: Given the first-order diagrammatic recursion relations for the running couplings $(a/T)$ and $(b/T)$ (assumed valid to first order in $\epsilon$) and the smallness of $\langle S^{2}\rangle=O(\epsilon)$ which justifies truncating the expansion after quartic terms, the algebraic procedure of setting $b=0$ and enforcing fixed-point invariance of the $k^{2}$ coefficient yields the short-range fixed-point coordinates $(T/a)^{*}=2\pi\epsilon/(n-2)$ and $\eta_{SR}=\epsilon/(n-2)$ to the order calculated; the logical algebraic steps from the recursion relations to these fixed-point expressions are straightforward and complete under the stated approximations.

Assuming the recursion relations (6) and (7) correctly capture the $k^{\sigma}$ sector at first order and that the analysis is carried out in the regime $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$, equating the $k^{\sigma}$ coefficients at a fixed point directly yields $\eta_{LR}=2-\sigma$; the step from the recursion relation to this equality is a direct matching of power-law momentum dependence and is logically tight given the premises.

Given that the SR fixed-point coordinates $(T/a)^{*}$ and $\eta_{SR}$ are correct ([@conclusion-2]) and that the multiplicative RG flow for $(T/a)$ at $b=0$ has the stated first-order form, linearizing that flow about $(T/a)^{*}$ and matching to the eigenvalue definition yields $\nu_{SR}=1/\epsilon+O(1)$; the linearization algebra and eigenvalue identification are standard and do not introduce further significant gaps beyond the premises. [@conclusion-2]

Assuming the SR fixed-point anomalous-dimension $\eta_{SR}$ is as stated ([@conclusion-2]) and the first-order recursion relations are valid, the linearization of the recursion relations in an infinitesimal long-range perturbation around the SR point yields the multiplicative factor $2^{2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}}$ for $\delta b/T$, and the translation of this eigenvalue into relevance/irrelevance (sign of $2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}$) is a straightforward and correct application of linear RG stability analysis. [@conclusion-2]

Given the LR fixed-point anomalous dimension result $\eta_{LR}=2-\sigma$ ([@conclusion-4]) and the SR anomalous dimension $\eta_{SR}$ ([@conclusion-2]), and assuming the $k^{\sigma}$ and $k^{2}$ sectors of the first-order recursion relations are correctly represented and the two-coupling truncation is valid to the order treated, the algebraic extraction of $(T/(a+b))^{*}=2\pi(d-\sigma)/(n-1)$ and $(b/a)^{*}=[(n-2)/(d-\sigma)](2-\sigma-\eta_{SR})$ follows by direct coefficient matching and substitution; the internal consistency check (elimination of $b^{*}$ reproducing $(T/a)^{*}$) further supports the algebraic manipulations, so the reasoning is sound conditional on the stated premises. [@conclusion-2],[@conclusion-4]

Assuming the LR fixed-point coordinates and $\eta_{LR}$ obtained in the upstream analysis ([@conclusion-4], [@conclusion-5]) are correct and that the multiplicative flow equation for $(T/b)$ has the stated first-order form, linearizing this flow about the LR fixed point and matching to the eigenvalue definition of $\nu$ gives $\nu_{LR}=1/(d-\sigma)+O(1)$; the algebraic linearization is standard, but the result depends on the accuracy of the prior fixed-point inputs and the specific form of the subleading correction term, so the conditional confidence is slightly lower than for simpler coefficient-matching steps. [@conclusion-4],[@conclusion-5]

Provided the linearized SR eigenvalue $2^{2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}}$ ([@conclusion-7]) and the LR fixed-point equality $\eta_{LR}=2-\sigma$ ([@conclusion-4]) hold and the SR anomalous dimension $\eta_{SR}$ is as calculated ([@conclusion-2]), the translation of the sign of $2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}$ into the two alternatives (SR stable when $\eta_{SR}>2-\sigma$, LR stable when $\eta_{SR}<2-\sigma$) and the identification of the dividing line $\sigma=2-\eta_{SR}$ as the boundary of domains of attraction is a direct application of linear RG stability theory and is logically sound under the stated premises. [@conclusion-7],[@conclusion-4],[@conclusion-2]
